title: Subjective Probability: A Judgment of Representativeness
delta_note: Global equivalence margin (standardized units). Cohen (1988) defines d < 0.2 as 'negligible' effect.

evaluation_framework.description: Two-tier evaluation: phenomenon-level and data-level matching

type_1.name: phenomenon_level_match
type_1.description: Tests whether agent exhibits representativeness heuristic (ignoring sample size)
type_1.approach: Uses proportion tests to verify agents judge representativeness over statistical validity

type_2.name: data_level_match
type_2.description: Tests how closely agent data matches human baseline data using equivalence testing
type_2.approach: TOST (Two One-Sided Tests) with Freeman-Tukey standardization for proportions
type_2.rationale: Tests H₁: |d| < δ (equivalence) rather than H₁: d ≠ 0 (difference). Lower p-value indicates stronger equivalence.

birth_sequence_problem.description: Families of 6 children birth order comparison
birth_sequence_problem.interpretation: Participants systematically judge BGBBBB as less likely than GBGBBG despite equal objective probability, demonstrating representativeness bias

program_problem.correct_answer_note: Higher variance at p=0.45 makes Program B more likely
program_problem.interpretation: Participants choose based on representativeness (majority match) rather than statistical likelihood (variance)

birth_sequence_effect.conclusion: Participants systematically judge less representative sequence as less likely despite equal objective probability

program_choice_effect.effect_interpretation: Strong representativeness bias overrides variance consideration
program_choice_effect.conclusion: Participants choose based on representativeness (majority preservation) rather than correct statistical reasoning

required_tests.description: Tests whether agent exhibits representativeness bias in birth sequence judgment

method.rationale: Proportion test showing bias significantly above chance (50%)

required_tests.description: Tests whether agent chooses based on representativeness over statistical correctness

method.rationale: Proportion test showing bias significantly above chance (50%)

required_tests.description: TOST equivalence test: agent bias proportion vs human baseline

method.rationale: Freeman-Tukey transformation: θ = arcsin(√p), SE = 1/(2√n). Tests if |θ_agent - θ_human| < δ.

required_tests.description: TOST equivalence test: agent choice proportion vs human baseline

data_level_scoring.rationale: Lower TOST p-value → stronger equivalence → higher score
data_level_scoring.p_value_interpretation: Raw p-values preserved for statistical rigor, no arbitrary thresholds

phenomenon_interpretation.P1_P2_any_fail: Agent does not exhibit representativeness heuristic (phenomenon absent)

combined_interpretation.P_pass_D_high: Strong replication: both phenomenon present and data match
combined_interpretation.P_pass_D_medium: Good replication: phenomenon present, moderate data match
combined_interpretation.P_pass_D_low: Weak replication: phenomenon present but different magnitude
combined_interpretation.P_fail: Failed replication: fundamental phenomenon not reproduced

key_findings.main_finding: People judge probability based on representativeness rather than statistical principles, systematically ignoring sample size and variance
key_findings.theoretical_implication: Demonstrates systematic deviation from normative (Bayesian) probability judgment
key_findings.practical_implication: Representativeness heuristic leads to predictable errors in probability estimation and decision making

notes.replication_notes: This is a foundational study in judgment and decision making. The representativeness heuristic is one of the most robust cognitive biases.
notes.implementation_notes: Present scenarios clearly and ensure agents understand the statistical principles to test whether they override them with representativeness judgments.
notes.common_issues: Some participants may correctly apply statistical reasoning. This is expected and indicates understanding rather than bias.
